title: Word Motions
subtitle: Move a word at a time. The grammar's first real noun.
summary: {key:w}/{key:b}/{key:e}/{key:g}{key:e} move forward and backward by word. They're motions, so they pair with operators: {key:d}{key:w} deletes a word, {key:c}{key:w} changes one, {key:y}{key:w} yanks one.

blocks.text: Moving one character at a time with {key:h}{key:j}{key:k}{key:l} is fine for surgery. For everything else you want to move *a word at a time*. That's what {key:w}, {key:b}, {key:e}, and {key:ge} do — and because they're motions, they double as the noun half of every editing command.

blocks.label: Forward to the start of the next word
Back to the start of the current/previous word
Forward to the end of the current/next word
Back to the end of the previous word

blocks.text: Counts work: {key:5}{key:w} jumps five words forward. So does {key:d}{key:5}{key:w} — delete five words. The grammar is consistent.

blocks.title: What Counts as a "Word"?
blocks.text:
Vim's notion of a word is governed by the `iskeyword` option (`:set iskeyword?` to see it). By default it includes letters, digits, and `_` — the things that look like an identifier in most languages.

A "word" is a maximal run of *keyword* characters, *or* a maximal run of *non-keyword non-whitespace* characters. So in `foo->bar`, the words are `foo`, `->`, and `bar`. {key:w} skips between all three.

If that's too granular for you, **WORD** ({key:W}, {key:B}, {key:E}, {key:gE}) treats anything not whitespace as part of the word. In `foo->bar`, that's a single WORD. See [*WORD Motions*](#wider-motions.WORD).
Mnemonic: lowercase = stops at punctuation; uppercase = whitespace only.

blocks.title: Word motions are a token-class scanner
blocks.text: {key:w}, {key:b}, {key:e}, {key:g}{key:e} aren't regex-driven; they classify each character into one of three token classes — word, punctuation, whitespace — and step through class boundaries. Empty lines count as a hard boundary regardless of class. The capitalized variants ({key:W}, {key:B}, {key:E}) collapse word and punctuation into a single class, so they only stop at whitespace. The implementation is one scanner with a swappable classifier.
